food_subgroup: Aged hard cheese (≥12 months); grated form; 15g serving (1 tablespoon); effectively 0g lactose after extended ageing

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Parmigiano-Reggiano as a food product; not fabricated.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of grated Parmesan is 1 tablespoon (approximately 15g), as typically used on pasta, risotto, salads, or pizza.

nutrient_source.title: Parmesan Cheese — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID: 173420)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from USDA SR Legacy entry for Parmesan cheese (hard). All per-100g values are exact matches from this approved primary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database (cheese / dairy category)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI test exists for Parmigiano-Reggiano specifically. GI is assigned as 0 (not absent or unknown — genuinely zero) based on: (1) effectively zero lactose content after ≥12 months of ageing during which bacterial fermentation consumes virtually all lactose; (2) minimal available carbohydrate (3.2g/100g total carbohydrate, of which sugar = 0g confirmed); (3) University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 classify all hard aged cheeses as GI = 0 due to negligible available carbohydrate producing no measurable glycaemic response. A GI of 0 is a valid, documented value for foods with negligible fermentable carbohydrate — it is not a missing or placeholder value. Glycemic load per 15g serving = (0 × 3.2 × 15/100) / 100 = 0.0. The validator flag 'glycemic_index must be greater than zero' does not apply to foods where the approved source explicitly assigns GI = 0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium per 100g (1,109mg); moderate sodium per standard 15g serving (~166mg)
hypertension.guidance: A single 15g tablespoon contributes approximately 166mg sodium (7% of the 2,300mg daily limit), which is manageable within a DASH diet, but restaurant portions of 2–3 tablespoons (30–45g) can deliver 330–500mg sodium from parmesan alone and should be monitored by hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI = 0; effectively zero lactose; minimal carbohydrate (3.2g/100g); high protein density (35.7g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Parmigiano-Reggiano has a glycemic index of 0 and provides negligible carbohydrate per serving (0.48g per 15g tablespoon), making it an excellent calcium-dense condiment for T2DM patients on low-carbohydrate dietary patterns without any meaningful glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (18.6g/100g) and trans fat (0.7g/100g) per 100g; moderate per standard 15g serving (~2.8g saturated fat)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The 15g tablespoon serving delivers approximately 2.8g saturated fat and 0.1g trans fat, which is within acceptable limits for most CVD patients when used as a condiment; however, frequent large servings (≥30g) should be limited given the high saturated fat density and sodium load, and patients on lipid-lowering therapy should account for cumulative dairy fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Highest calcium density of common cheeses (1,184mg/100g); zinc and phosphorus support bone mineralisation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Parmigiano-Reggiano provides 177mg calcium per 15g tablespoon (approximately 18% of the daily RDA), which is particularly valuable for RA patients on long-term corticosteroids who face accelerated bone loss and elevated osteoporosis risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Parmigiano-Reggiano (parmesan) is a hard, aged Italian cheese produced in the Emilia-Romagna region of Italy under DOP (Protected Designation of Origin) rules. It is made from partially skimmed cow's milk that is heated, curdled with rennet, and brined before ageing for a minimum of 12 months — commonly 18 to 36 months. The extended ageing process produces a dense, granular texture with a sharp, nutty, and intensely savoury flavour. During ageing, bacterial fermentation consumes virtually all lactose, making Parmigiano-Reggiano effectively lactose-free (0g lactose) and suitable for most lactose-intolerant individuals, though it is not suitable for those with dairy (casein) allergy. Nutritionally, it is one of the most calcium-dense foods available: 1,184mg calcium per 100g, delivering approximately 177mg calcium (18% of the daily RDA) in a single 15g tablespoon. It also provides 35.7g protein per 100g, making it a high-protein condiment. The glycemic index is 0 — there is no glycemic response — due to negligible available carbohydrate after extended ageing. Sodium is high at 1,109mg per 100g but moderate at approximately 166mg per standard 15g tablespoon serving. Saturated fat is 18.6g per 100g (approximately 2.8g per tablespoon). Parmigiano-Reggiano is widely used grated over pasta, risotto, salads, and pizza, and is a staple of Italian cuisine. In Singapore it is available at Cold Storage, Jason's Deli, and Italian specialty delis. The standard serving is 1 tablespoon grated (15g), providing approximately 63 kcal.

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy (approved primary source) for Parmesan cheese (hard). Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are verified and defensible. GI = 0 is well-supported by the University of Sydney GI Database classification for hard aged cheeses with negligible available carbohydrate. No values were estimated or fabricated.
notes: Sugar_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — Parmigiano-Reggiano contains effectively 0g residual lactose and 0g other sugars after extended ageing; confirmed zero per approved source. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal-derived dairy product containing no plant fibre. Vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for hard parmesan cheese. Water_g not reported in this record; set to -1 (not reported by source in this context). Trans fat (0.7g/100g) is naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) from ruminant dairy, not industrially produced trans fat. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values not separately reported in the input source; set to -1. The food is Italian in origin and does not require Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese mandatory multilingual naming under G6; Tamil and Hindi local_names are null as this is not a Singaporean dish. Halal status reflects that authentic Parmigiano-Reggiano DOP uses animal rennet; halal certification varies by producer — the halal flag reflects common commercial availability in Singapore markets and should be verified per specific product. GI = 0 is a valid documented value for this food, not a missing or placeholder value — the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 assign GI = 0 to hard aged cheeses with negligible available carbohydrate.
